events.label: reasoning
events.text:
The scenario: PVC flue slope back-pitched (condensing) on a commercial furnace venting system. This is a defect - condensing furnace flue pipes must slope back toward the furnace (or toward a drain) to allow condensate to drain properly. A "back-pitched" slope means the pipe is sloped the wrong way, which would cause condensate to pool or flow back toward the vent termination rather than draining properly. This can lead to condensate accumulation, potential flue gas spillage, corrosion, and safety hazards.

Let me assess:
- Decision: fail (non-compliant and defective)
- Risk: high (potential for flue gas spillage, carbon monoxide, equipment damage)
- Work stage: in-progress
- Component conditions: installed-defective, non-compliant
- Percent complete: estimate based on in-progress work, maybe 40%
- Findings: PVC flue pipe back-pitched, condensate will pool, potential flue gas blockage
- Actions: re-pitch flue to proper slope (1/4" per foot toward furnace/drain), support properly
- References: IRC M1801, NFPA 54

    "Stop work on the affected flue section immediately",
    "Re-pitch the PVC flue pipe to slope continuously toward the furnace or approved condensate drain at minimum 1/4 inch per foot",
    "Verify all hangers and supports are spaced and adjusted to maintain correct slope with no sags",
    "Inspect the full flue run for additional back-pitch or low points and correct as needed",
    "Re-inspect and document slope compliance before resuming work or calling for third-party inspection"
  ],
